heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2955
card_summary: This final chunk of the Bompiani 2010 edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi contains the tail end of the Index of Biblical Passages (Indice dei Passi Biblici), covering the concluding verses of the Book of Revelation (chapters 20-22). Each verse citation is cross-referenced to the specific canticle, tone, and chapter of Zorzi's tripartite structure (e.g., 3,8,2,2 or 1,6,19). The chunk also includes a publisher's note from the Fondazione Arnone–Bellavite Pellegrini and its Centre for Science, Philosophy and Language Research (CeSPhiLaR), describing the broader editorial series "Classici dell'Umanesimo e del Rinascimento" in which this edition appears. That series description names dozens of Renaissance and medieval thinkers — from Ficino and Pico della Mirandola to Machiavelli, Erasmus, and Paracelsus — situating Zorzi within the Platonic-Neoplatonic tradition alongside Cusanus and Giordano Bruno. The Kabbalah and Leone Ebreo are specifically named among the religious-philosophical currents the series addresses.
essay:
## Index of Biblical Passages: Final Entries and Editorial Apparatus

### Nature and Function of the Index

The *Indice dei Passi Biblici* (Index of Biblical Passages) is a scholarly finding-aid appended to Saverio Campanini's critical edition of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Bompiani, 2010, "Il Pensiero Occidentale" series). As an index of scriptural loci, it records every biblical verse that Zorzi cites, quotes, or alludes to across the entire three-canticle work, and maps each citation to the precise internal address within Zorzi's complex nested structure — canticle, tone (or book subdivision), chapter, and section number. The index thus functions as a reverse concordance: rather than moving from Zorzi's text outward to Scripture, it allows a reader to begin with a given verse and locate all of Zorzi's engagements with it.

### Organization

The index is organized canonically — that is, by the standard order of books of the Bible — and within each book, sequentially by chapter and verse. Each entry takes the form of a verse reference (book chapter:verse) followed by one or more location codes in the format `[canticle].[tone].[chapter].[section]`. Multiple citations of the same verse are separated by semicolons. The index is unparagraphed and set in dense double-column format across several pages, maximizing reference utility at the cost of readability.

### Scholarly Value

For researchers in Renaissance Christian Kabbalah, the index is indispensable. Zorzi's entire project in *De Harmonia Mundi* is to demonstrate the harmonic consonance of Scripture, Neoplatonic cosmology, and Kabbalistic number mysticism. The biblical index reveals at a glance which scriptural passages bear the heaviest exegetical weight in his system. The concentration of Revelation citations visible in this final chunk — particularly from chapters 21 and 22, the vision of the New Jerusalem — confirms that Zorzi's eschatological and architectural symbolism (the twelve gates, the measuring of the city, the river of life) is among his most densely cited material, appearing repeatedly across all three canticles. The index therefore makes legible the deep structure of Zorzi's biblical theology in a way that even close reading of the main text cannot easily supply.

### Scope of the Final Chunk

Chunk 163 preserves only the final portion of the index, covering **Revelation 20:2 through 22:17**. This is the apocalyptic conclusion of the Christian biblical canon, and its density of cross-references in Zorzi's text is notable: Revelation 21 alone generates citations to dozens of separate loci in the *De Harmonia Mundi*, touching all three canticles and many tones. Revelation 22, the very last chapter of the Bible, is also well-represented. The final indexed verse visible in the chunk is Revelation 22:17.

### Sample Entries (verbatim from the index)

The following entries are reproduced exactly as they appear in the text:

1. **Rev. 21,2** — `1,7,35; 2,5,13; 3,8,12,3; 3,8,4,1; 3,8,5,1; 3,8,5,2`
   *(The vision of the New Jerusalem descending as a bride — one of the most multiply-cited verses in the entire index, engaged across both canticles 1, 2, and 3.)*

2. **Rev. 21,12** — `1,6,19; 1,8,18; 2,7,7; 3,3,7; 3,8,1,3`
   *(The twelve gates of the heavenly city, each bearing a name of the tribes of Israel — central to Zorzi's Kabbalistic numerology.)*

3. **Rev. 21,22** — `1,7,33; 3,8,2,1`
   *(The temple-less city, where God and the Lamb are themselves the temple — relevant to Zorzi's theology of divine immanence.)*

4. **Rev. 22,2** — `1,6,19; 1,7,7; 2,3,6; 2,7,6; 2,8,2; 3,1,5; 3,8,2,5; 3,8,10,4`
   *(The tree of life bearing twelve fruits — one of the most extensively cited verses in the entire final section, appearing across all three canticles and multiple tones.)*

5. **Rev. 21,5** — `2,proem.; 2,2,12; 3,7,6; 3,7,7`
   *(The divine proclamation "Behold, I make all things new" — cited even in the proem of the second canticle, indicating its structural importance to Zorzi's overall compositional program.)*

### Publisher's Colophon

Following the index entries, the chunk includes a substantial note from the **Fondazione Arnone–Bellavite Pellegrini** and its Centre for Science, Philosophy and Language Research (CeSPhiLaR), which co-sponsors the "Classici dell'Umanesimo e del Rinascimento" series under which this edition appears. The note situates Zorzi within a panoramic intellectual genealogy running from Carolingian and Arab-Norman-Swabian medieval revivals through the full arc of the Italian Renaissance and beyond, naming Ficino, Pico della Mirandola, Cusanus, Bruno, Campanella, Machiavelli, Erasmus, Paracelsus, and — significantly for Zorzi's own tradition — the Kabbalah and Leone Ebreo among the currents the series is committed to restoring to contemporary scholarly attention. This colophon underscores the editorial ambition of Campanini's edition: to re-insert Zorzi into the living canon of Renaissance thought rather than treating him as a marginal curiosity.
